summary: Do not ship B-1: caller cancellation lets the new uncancellable commit escape same-key serialization, so an expired upload can overwrite or resurrect state after a later successful operation.

findings.title: Uncancellable pointer commits outlive the per-key lock
findings.body: `put_stream` keeps `_g` in the caller future but transfers only `PinGuard` into `commit_pointer`. The HTTP upload timeout drops that caller future and releases `_g`, while the deliberately non-abortable blocking closure may still be staging or waiting to rename. A retry or delete for the same bucket/key can then acquire the lock and finish first; when the older closure resumes, it can overwrite the newer pointer or recreate a successfully deleted key. The planned B-2 tests exercise GC settlement, not this same-key ordering failure, so continuing would cement an unsafe commit API.
findings.recommendation: Keep the load-bearing uncancellable `PinGuard` ownership, but make the commit closure also own the per-key `OwnedMutexGuard`—preferably through an opaque commit token—until rename and fsync finish. Add a deterministic pre-rename cancellation test proving a same-key retry/delete remains blocked and ultimately wins.

next_steps: Revise the commit seam so all ordering guards outlive the uncancellable operation.
Add the cancellation-order regression, refresh B-1 verification evidence, and repeat the structure review.
